Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Nabarangapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 35
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Shoe Maker and Related Workers, Other
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Nabarangapur
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: Kishor Maharana is a 35-year-old male from Nabarangapur, Odisha, who works as a shoemaker with primary education and is married. He speaks Odia as his first language and lives in a rural area, managing his workshop with basic bookkeeping. His lifestyle includes following cricket, enjoying traditional Odia folk music, and cooking local dishes like dalma and chhena poda.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ବର୍ତ୍ତମାନର ଅସୁସ୍ଥତାର ଇତିହାସ: ଶ୍ରୀ କିଶୋର ମହାରଣା, ଜଣେ 35 ବର୍ଷର ପୁରୁଷ ଚମାର, ତିନି ମାସର ବ୍ୟାପକ ଦୁଃଖ, anhedonia ଏବଂ ଅଦରକାରୀ ହେବାର ଅନୁଭବ ସହିତ ଉପସ୍ଥାପିତ ହୋଇଛନ୍ତି। ସେ ଜଣାଇଛନ୍ତି ଯେ ତାଙ୍କ କାର୍ଯ୍ୟର ଉତ୍ପାଦକତାରେ ଏକ ଗୁରୁତ୍ୱପୂର୍ଣ୍ଣ ହ୍ରାସ ଘଟିଛି ଏବଂ ସେ ତାଙ୍କର ସଉକଗୁଡ଼ିକରେ ଅଂଶଗ୍ରହଣ କରିବା ବନ୍ଦ କରିଦେଇଛନ୍ତି, ଯେଉଁଥିରେ ଓଡ଼ିଆ ଲୋକସଙ୍ଗୀତ ଶୁଣିବା ଅନ୍ତର୍ଭୁକ୍ତ। ସେ ସକାଳୁ ଶୀଘ୍ର ଉଠିବା ଅନୁଭବ କରନ୍ତି ଏବଂ ଦିନସାରା କ୍ଲାନ୍ତ ଅନୁଭବ କରନ୍ତି। କୌଣସି ମାନସିକ ବିକୃତିର ଲକ୍ଷଣ ରିପୋର୍ଟ କରାଯାଇ ନାହିଁ।
ଅତୀତର ମାନସିକ ରୋଗର ଇତିହାସ: ପୂର୍ବରୁ କୌଣସି ମାନସିକ ରୋଗର ନିଦାନ କିମ୍ବା ଚିକିତ୍ସା ହୋଇନାହିଁ।
ଅତୀତର ଚିକିତ୍ସା ଇତିହାସ: କୌଣସି ଗୁରୁତ୍ୱପୂର୍ଣ୍ଣ ଚିକିତ୍ସା ରୋଗ ନାହିଁ।
ଔଷଧ: କିଛି ନାହିଁ।
ପରିବାର ଇତିହାସ: ଅବଦାନହୀନ।
ସାମାଜିକ ଇତିହାସ: ରୋଗୀ ବିବାହିତ ଏବଂ ଏକ ଗ୍ରାମାଞ୍ଚଳରେ ରହନ୍ତି. ସେ ଜଣେ ଚମାର ଭାବରେ କାମ କରନ୍ତି ଏବଂ ଏକ ଛୋଟ କର୍ମଶାଳା ପରିଚାଳନା କରନ୍ତି. ସେ କୌଣସି ନିଶାଦ୍ରବ୍ୟ ବ୍ୟବହାର କରନ୍ତି ନାହିଁ।
ପରୀକ୍ଷା:
ମାନସିକ ସ୍ଥିତି ପରୀକ୍ଷା: ରୋଗୀ ସଚେତନ ଏବଂ ଅଭିମୁଖୀ ଅଟନ୍ତି. ତାଙ୍କ ମନୋଭାବ ନିମ୍ନ, ଭାବପ୍ରକାଶ ସୀମିତ ଏବଂ କଥାବାର୍ତ୍ତା ସାଧାରଣ ଗତି ଏବଂ ସ୍ୱରରେ ଅଛି. ଚିନ୍ତାଧାରା ଯୁକ୍ତିଯୁକ୍ତ ଏବଂ କୌଣସି ଆନୁଷ୍ଠାନିକ ଚିନ୍ତାଧାରା ବ୍ୟାଧି ନାହିଁ. ଅନ୍ତର୍ଦୃଷ୍ଟି ଏବଂ ବିଚାର ଶକ୍ତି ଦୁର୍ବଳ ଅଟେ।
ଶାରୀରିକ ପରୀକ୍ଷା: ସାଧାରଣ ସୀମା ମଧ୍ୟରେ।
ନିଦାନ: Major Depressive Disorder, Single Episode, Moderate.
ଚିକିତ୍ସା ଯୋଜନା:
1. ଆରମ୍ଭ କରନ୍ତୁ Sertraline 50mg ଦିନକୁ ଥରେ, ଏକ ସପ୍ତାହ ପରେ ସହନଶୀଳତା ଅନୁଯାୟୀ 100mg କୁ ବୃଦ୍ଧି କରାଯିବ।
2. ରୋଗୀ ଏବଂ ତାଙ୍କ ପତ୍ନୀଙ୍କୁ ଅବସାଦ, ଚିକିତ୍ସା ଅନୁପାଳନ ଏବଂ ଅନୁସରଣର ଗୁରୁତ୍ୱ ବିଷୟରେ ମନୋ-ଶିକ୍ଷା ପ୍ରଦାନ କରନ୍ତୁ।
3. ନିୟମିତ ନିଦ୍ରା ସ୍ୱଚ୍ଛତା ଏବଂ ହାଲୁକା ଶାରୀରିକ କାର୍ଯ୍ୟକଳାପରେ ଅଂଶଗ୍ରହଣ ପାଇଁ ପରାମର୍ଶ ଦିଅନ୍ତୁ।
4. ଚିକିତ୍ସା ପ୍ରତିକ୍ରିୟାର ଆକଳନ ଏବଂ ପାର୍ଶ୍ୱ ପ୍ରତିକ୍ରିୟା ପାଇଁ ଦୁଇ ସପ୍ତାହ ପରେ ଏକ ଅନୁସରଣ ସାକ୍ଷାତକାର ସ୍ଥିର କରନ୍ତୁ।
5. ସହାୟକ ମନୋଚିକିତ୍ସା ଅଧିବେଶନ ପାଇଁ ପରାମର୍ଶ କେନ୍ଦ୍ରକୁ ପଠାନ୍ତୁ।

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Nabarangapur District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|NDH-2024-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Kishor Maharana]] | DOB [[DOB|1989-08-22]] | Age: [[AGE|35]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Shoe Maker and Related Workers, Other]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0822-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. K. Patro]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sushila Maharana]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9438765432]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-5123]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Nabarangapur]]
Chief complaint: Patient reports persistent low mood, loss of interest in daily activities, and difficulty sleeping for the past three months.
History of present illness: Mr. Kishor Maharana, a 35-year-old male shoemaker, presents with a three-month history of pervasive sadness, anhedonia, and feelings of worthlessness. He reports a significant decrease in his work productivity and has stopped engaging in his hobbies, including listening to Odia folk music. He endorses early morning awakening and feels fatigued throughout the day. There are no reported psychotic symptoms.
Past psychiatric history: No prior psychiatric diagnosis or treatment.
Past medical history: No significant medical illnesses.
Medications: None.
Family history: Non-contributory.
Social history: The patient is married and lives in a rural village. He works as a shoemaker and manages a small workshop. He denies any substance use.
Examination:
Mental state examination: The patient is conscious and oriented. His mood is low, affect is constricted, and speech is of normal rate and volume. Thought process is logical, and there are no formal thought disorders. Insight and judgment are impaired.
Physical examination: Within normal limits.
Diagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder, Single Episode, Moderate.
Treatment plan:
1. Initiate treatment with Sertraline 50mg once daily, to be increased to 100mg after one week as tolerated.
2. Provide psychoeducation to the patient and his wife regarding the nature of depression, treatment adherence, and the importance of follow-up.
3. Advise regular sleep hygiene and engagement in light physical activity.
4. Schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to assess response to treatment and side effects.
5. Refer to the counseling center for supportive psychotherapy sessions.
Follow-up: [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0912-01]]

professional_persona: Kishor Maharana is a 35‑year‑old master craftsman specializing in hand‑crafted footwear, blending traditional leather stitching with modest design tweaks, and they manage their workshop with diligent bookkeeping, reliable customer service, and a practical curiosity that drives them to explore new tools and expand into nearby towns and online platforms.
linguistic_persona: Kishor Maharana primarily communicates in Odia, comfortably reading and writing in it, and they possess functional Hindi for official dealings while their exposure to English is limited to trade‑specific jargon and a few borrowed terms.
sports_persona: Kishor Maharana follows cricket passionately on the radio, roots for Chennai Super Kings in the IPL, and joins occasional village‑level cricket matches and kabaddi games, using them as a lively outlet for fitness and camaraderie.
arts_persona: Kishor Maharana revels in Odia folk songs and bhajans, often tapping the dholak to tunes by Akshaya Mohanty and Prafulla Kar, while also enjoying Bollywood blockbusters starring Amitabh Bachchan and Shah Rukh Khan, and they occasionally attend Odissi dance showcases during village festivals.
travel_persona: Kishor Maharana prefers budget bus trips to nearby Puri for Rath Yatra and Bhubaneswar for market deals, occasionally journeys to Kolkata by train, and dreams of a pilgrimage to Delhi’s historic forts, planning each outing through word‑of‑mouth and modest mobile apps.
culinary_persona: Kishor Maharana enjoys cooking traditional Odia fare like dalma, chakuli pitha, and roti, seasoning with mustard seeds, ginger and coconut, and they love serving sweet treats such as chhena poda and rasgulla to family and neighbours during festivals.
persona: Kishor Maharana is a practical yet curious village shoemaker who balances tradition with modest innovation, values community harmony above all, and quietly juggles workshop duties, family hopes, and a habit of lingering over paperwork.
cultural_background: Born and raised in the rural village of Nabarangapur, Odisha, Kishor Maharana hails from a traditional Odia artisan community where hand-crafted footwear has been passed down through generations. His cultural life revolves around village festivals like Rath Yatra, Nuakhai and local worship of village deities, as well as communal storytelling and folk music. While deeply rooted in these customs, his innate curiosity leads him to experiment with modest design innovations, reflecting a practical blend of tradition and modernity.
linguistic_background: Kishor’s first language is Odia, which he speaks, reads and writes with confidence, allowing him to manage day‑to‑day transactions, follow local news and keep records for his workshop. He has a basic functional understanding of Hindi, mainly for official communication and occasional market dealings, but he does not read or write Hindi fluently. His schooling only went up to primary level, so his exposure to English is minimal, limited to a few borrowed terms used in the trade.
skills_and_expertise: Kishor is skilled in hand‑crafted shoe making, including leather cutting, pattern drafting, hand stitching, and finishing. He can design simple styles that suit local preferences and repair worn footwear efficiently. His practical mindset ensures consistent quality, while his curiosity pushes him to experiment with modest design tweaks. He also manages the day‑to‑day operations of his small workshop, handling inventory, basic bookkeeping, and direct customer service with a cooperative and reliable approach.

hobbies_and_interests: In his free time, Kishor enjoys listening to Odia folk songs and bhajans, occasionally playing the dholak during village gatherings. He follows cricket matches on the radio and likes to watch short tutorial videos on shoe design on his mobile phone. Gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables, offers him quiet relaxation, while cooking traditional Odia dishes such as dalma and roti lets him share meals with family and neighbours. He also participates in local fairs and helps organize community events.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['listening to Odia folk music', 'playing dholak', 'watching cricket', 'viewing shoe design videos', 'gardening', 'cooking traditional Odia dishes', 'participating in village fairs', 'helping organize community events']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Kishor aims to grow his modest shoe‑making workshop into a more organized small enterprise that can supply nearby towns and potentially tap online platforms. He plans to learn basic modern tailoring tools, improve design variety, and train a couple of apprentices from his village. By increasing his income, he hopes to provide higher‑level education for his children and secure a comfortable retirement for himself, all while preserving the traditional craft and maintaining the harmonious village life he values.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +

Language SLA:

Date: 15 August 2024

Patient Information:
The patient is a 35-year-old male from Nabarangapur district, Odisha, working as a shoe maker. He presents with persistent cough for 3 weeks, low-grade fever, and weight loss of 5 kg. No history of diabetes or hypertension. Non-smoker, occasional alcohol use.

Laboratory Findings:

Sputum Examination:
- AFB Smear: Positive (2+ bacilli per 100 fields)
- Culture: Positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis (susceptible to first-line drugs)

Complete Blood Count:
- Hemoglobin: 11.2 g/dL
- WBC Count: 9,800/cmm
- Neutrophils: 65%
- Lymphocytes: 25%
- ESR: 45 mm/hr

Biochemistry:
- Blood Sugar (Fasting): 98 mg/dL
- Blood Sugar (PP): 126 mg/dL
- Total Cholesterol: 198 mg/dL
- LDL: 120 mg/dL
- HDL: 38 mg/dL
- Triglycerides: 165 mg/dL

Liver Function Tests:
- SGOT: 45 U/L
- SGPT: 52 U/L
- Total Bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL

Kidney Function Tests:
- Serum Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
- Blood Urea: 22 mg/dL

Chest X-ray:
- Bilateral upper zone infiltrates with cavitation in right upper lobe

Interpretation:
The patient has active pulmonary tuberculosis with associated anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. Lipid profile shows borderline dyslipidemia. No significant liver or renal dysfunction noted.

Treatment Plan:
Start DOTS regimen for tuberculosis:
- Isoniazid 300mg daily
- Rifampicin 600mg daily
- Pyrazinamide 1500mg daily
- Ethambutol 800mg daily

Follow-up:
- Repeat sputum examination after 2 months
- Monitor liver function tests monthly
- Lipid profile after 3 months
- Nutritional support and counseling

She designs eye‑catching stall displays using locally sourced materials and adapts quickly to weather changes, embodying *jugaad* ingenuity. Rekha also excels in building rapport with regular customers, remembering their preferences, and offering personalized suggestions. Her compassionate nature extends to mediating disputes among fellow vendors and assisting neighbors with small errands, reflecting strong community mediation abilities. Additionally, she possesses basic first‑aid knowledge useful in emergencies at the bustling market.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Ganjam.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Street Vendors, Non Food Products, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Ganjam
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old female street vendor in rural Ganjam, Odisha, who is illiterate and speaks Odia. She is currently married and has no formal education. Her lifestyle includes mental arithmetic for business, frequent walking to the market, and following local cricket and kabaddi. She also sings folk songs and creates rangoli during festivals.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

judge_reasoning: Odia prose and allowed entity tags match expectations; ECT note fits psychiatry domain and 45F persona with no mismatches or invented types.

professional_persona: Rekha Naik is a seasoned street vendor of non‑food items in Ganjam, known for shrewd bargaining, mental arithmetic, and inventive stall displays that blend practicality with a touch of *jugaad*, and they constantly seek to expand their product range to boost earnings.
linguistic_persona: Rekha Naik speaks fluent Odia, can follow simple Hindi when needed, relies on oral storytelling and sharp mental calculations to run the stall, and they manage all business dealings without ever reading or writing a single line.
sports_persona: Rekha Naik enjoys early morning walks to the market, follows the Odisha cricket team and the Indian Premier League's Sunrisers Hyderabad on TV, occasionally watches local kabaddi tournaments, and they occasionally join a community women's volleyball group for light exercise.
arts_persona: Rekha Naik loves singing Odia folk songs, especially those popularized by Akshaya Mohanty, creates vibrant rangoli designs during festivals, watches beloved Odia films starring Anubhav Mohanty, and they regularly tune into the local radio for folk music and community news.
travel_persona: Rekha Naik travels by bus to nearby towns for supplies, makes an annual pilgrimage to Puri for the Jagannath Rath Yatra, occasionally visits Bhubaneswar for healthcare, and they dream of a future trip to the sun‑kissed beaches of Goa and the historic Konark Sun Temple.
culinary_persona: Rekha Naik delights in preparing traditional Odia dishes like pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda, favoring ingredients such as mustard seeds, turmeric, coconut and fresh ginger, and they often relish the tangy kick of mango pickle alongside the meals.
persona: Rekha Naik balances shrewd street‑selling instincts with a folk‑singer's heart, and they juggle mental math, spontaneous rangoli, and a habit of misplacing spare change after a busy market day.
cultural_background: Rekha Naik grew up in the rural heart of Ganjam district in Odisha, a region where the coastal breeze meets agrarian fields. Her cultural world is shaped by Odia traditions, the rhythm of monsoon farming, and vibrant village festivals like Raja, Durga Puja, and the annual Jagannath fair. As a Hindu, she follows family‑centred rituals, makes offerings at the local Shiva temple, and partakes in communal feasts that reinforce social bonds. The street‑vendor community she belongs to operates on trust, informal credit, and the ever‑present spirit of *jugaad*, where practical improvisation meets traditional values.
linguistic_background: Rekha’s mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily transactions, negotiations with customers, and interactions within the village. Because she never attended formal schooling, she is illiterate and cannot read or write in any language. She can understand simple spoken Hindi when traders pass by, but she does not use it for communication. Her linguistic skills rely heavily on oral storytelling, memory of product names, and mental calculation of prices.
skills_and_expertise: Rekha has honed a suite of practical skills that make her a successful street vendor of non‑food items. She is a shrewd negotiator, capable of securing low wholesale prices from itinerant suppliers through informal bargaining. Her mental arithmetic allows her to calculate costs, profits, and give correct change without any written ledger. She designs eye‑catching stall displays using locally sourced materials and adapts quickly to weather changes, embodying *jugaad* ingenuity. Rekha also excels in building rapport with regular customers, remembering their preferences, and offering personalized suggestions. Her compassionate nature extends to mediating disputes among fellow vendors and assisting neighbors with small errands, reflecting strong community mediation abilities. Additionally, she possesses basic first‑aid knowledge useful in emergencies at the bustling market.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Negotiation with suppliers', 'Mental arithmetic and cash handling', 'Impromptu stall design using local materials', 'Customer relationship management', 'Community mediation', 'Basic first aid', 'Knowledge of local market trends', 'Improvisation (jugaad) for weather adaptation']
hobbies_and_interests: Rekha enjoys singing traditional Odia folk songs, especially during festivals, and takes pleasure in creating colourful rangoli patterns on the courtyard floor. She tends to her modest vegetable garden, where she grows leafy greens and herbs used in everyday cooking. Cooking Odia dishes such as pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda is both a hobby and a way to share meals with neighbors. In her downtime she watches regional Odia movies and listens to the local radio for music and news. Participation in community festivals, dance events, and occasional craft workshops keeps her socially connected. She also likes to browse market stalls for new, affordable gadgets that could enhance her vending business.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing (Odia)', 'Rangoli making', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Cooking Odia dishes (pakhala, dalma, chhena poda)', 'Watching Odia movies', 'Listening to local radio', 'Participating in community festivals', 'Exploring affordable market gadgets']
career_goals_and_ambitions: To increase her daily turnover by about 30 % within the next two years, Rekha plans to diversify her product range with affordable household items and seasonal ornaments. She aims to secure a modest but permanent stall space in the local market, reducing dependence on temporary roadside spots. She wishes to train younger relatives or apprentices, passing on vendor skills to ensure family income stability. In the long term, she hopes to save enough to buy a small shop or a piece of land for a family home, while continuing to serve her community with honesty and compassion.
occupation: Street Vendors, Non Food Products, Other
persona_clinical_summary: A 45-year-old female from Ganjam, Odisha, who is currently married and works as a street vendor selling non-food products. She is illiterate, speaks Odia as her first language, and resides in a rural area. Her lifestyle includes mental arithmetic for business, community interactions, and a habit of misplacing small items.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. Patnaik]]
Patient presents with a chief complaint of generalized body ache and fatigue for the past two weeks, associated with an intermittent dry cough. She reports that the pain is diffuse, affecting her limbs and back, and is most severe in the mornings. She has been self-medicating with paracetamol tablets from a local medical shop with minimal relief. There is no history of fever, weight loss, or night sweats. She works as a street vendor selling non-food items and attributes her symptoms to overwork and exposure to dust.

Investigations ordered include a Complete Blood Count (CBC), Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR), and a basic metabolic panel.

Laboratory results indicate a hemoglobin level of 9.8 g/dL, suggesting mild normocytic anemia. The total leukocyte count is within normal limits. The ESR is elevated at 45 mm/hr. Liver function tests and renal function tests are reported as normal.

Impression:
1.  Mild normocytic anemia with fatigue.
2.  Elevated ESR.

Plan:
1.  Prescribed oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg and folic acid 5 mg tablets once daily.
2.  Advised to increase dietary intake of iron-rich foods such as spinach, lentils, and jaggery.
3.  Recommended adequate rest and avoidance of dust exposure at the workplace.
4.  Follow-up appointment scheduled in one week to review symptoms and repeat hemoglobin level.

professional_persona: Rekha Naik is a seasoned street vendor of non‑food items in Ganjam, known for shrewd bargaining, mental arithmetic, and inventive stall displays that blend practicality with a touch of *jugaad*, and they constantly seek to expand their product range to boost earnings.

sports_persona: Rekha Naik enjoys early morning walks to the market, follows the Odisha cricket team and the Indian Premier League's Sunrisers Hyderabad on TV, occasionally watches local kabaddi tournaments, and they occasionally join a community women's volleyball group for light exercise.
arts_persona: Rekha Naik loves singing Odia folk songs, especially those popularized by Akshaya Mohanty, creates vibrant rangoli designs during festivals, watches beloved Odia films starring Anubhav Mohanty, and they regularly tune into the local radio for folk music and community news.
travel_persona: Rekha Naik travels by bus to nearby towns for supplies, makes an annual pilgrimage to Puri for the Jagannath Rath Yatra, occasionally visits Bhubaneswar for healthcare, and they dream of a future trip to the sun‑kissed beaches of Goa and the historic Konark Sun Temple.
culinary_persona: Rekha Naik delights in preparing traditional Odia dishes like pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda, favoring ingredients such as mustard seeds, turmeric, coconut and fresh ginger, and they often relish the tangy kick of mango pickle alongside the meals.
persona: Rekha Naik balances shrewd street‑selling instincts with a folk‑singer's heart, and they juggle mental math, spontaneous rangoli, and a habit of misplacing spare change after a busy market day.
cultural_background: Rekha Naik grew up in the rural heart of Ganjam district in Odisha, a region where the coastal breeze meets agrarian fields. Her cultural world is shaped by Odia traditions, the rhythm of monsoon farming, and vibrant village festivals like Raja, Durga Puja, and the annual Jagannath fair. As a Hindu, she follows family‑centred rituals, makes offerings at the local Shiva temple, and partakes in communal feasts that reinforce social bonds. The street‑vendor community she belongs to operates on trust, informal credit, and the ever‑present spirit of *jugaad*, where practical improvisation meets traditional values.
linguistic_background: Rekha’s mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily transactions, negotiations with customers, and interactions within the village. Because she never attended formal schooling, she is illiterate and cannot read or write in any language. She can understand simple spoken Hindi when traders pass by, but she does not use it for communication. Her linguistic skills rely heavily on oral storytelling, memory of product names, and mental calculation of prices.
skills_and_expertise: Rekha has honed a suite of practical skills that make her a successful street vendor of non‑food items. She is a shrewd negotiator, capable of securing low wholesale prices from itinerant suppliers through informal bargaining. Her mental arithmetic allows her to calculate costs, profits, and give correct change without any written ledger. She designs eye‑catching stall displays using locally sourced materials and adapts quickly to weather changes, embodying *jugaad* ingenuity. Rekha also excels in building rapport with regular customers, remembering their preferences, and offering personalized suggestions. Her compassionate nature extends to mediating disputes among fellow vendors and assisting neighbors with small errands, reflecting strong community mediation abilities. Additionally, she possesses basic first‑aid knowledge useful in emergencies at the bustling market.

hobbies_and_interests: Rekha enjoys singing traditional Odia folk songs, especially during festivals, and takes pleasure in creating colourful rangoli patterns on the courtyard floor. She tends to her modest vegetable garden, where she grows leafy greens and herbs used in everyday cooking. Cooking Odia dishes such as pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda is both a hobby and a way to share meals with neighbors. In her downtime she watches regional Odia movies and listens to the local radio for music and news. Participation in community festivals, dance events, and occasional craft workshops keeps her socially connected. She also likes to browse market stalls for new, affordable gadgets that could enhance her vending business.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing (Odia)', 'Rangoli making', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Cooking Odia dishes (pakhala, dalma, chhena poda)', 'Watching Odia movies', 'Listening to local radio', 'Participating in community festivals', 'Exploring affordable market gadgets']
career_goals_and_ambitions: To increase her daily turnover by about 30 % within the next two years, Rekha plans to diversify her product range with affordable household items and seasonal ornaments. She aims to secure a modest but permanent stall space in the local market, reducing dependence on temporary roadside spots. She wishes to train younger relatives or apprentices, passing on vendor skills to ensure family income stability. In the long term, she hopes to save enough to buy a small shop or a piece of land for a family home, while continuing to serve her community with honesty and compassion.

persona_clinical_summary: A 45-year-old female street vendor in rural Ganjam, Odisha, who is currently married and illiterate, primarily speaks Odia. Her lifestyle includes daily market activities, mental arithmetic for sales, and a habit of misplacing small items. She has no formal education and relies on practical, community-based interactions.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Ganjam.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Street Vendors, Non Food Products, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Ganjam
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old female street vendor in rural Ganjam, Odisha, who is currently married and illiterate, primarily speaks Odia. Her lifestyle includes daily market activities, mental arithmetic for sales, and a habit of misplacing small items. She has no formal education and relies on practical, community-based interactions.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ଡାକ୍ତର [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. K. Patnaik]]
ରୋଗୀ ଜଣକ Chatrapur, Ganjam ର ଜଣେ 45 ବର୍ଷର ନିରକ୍ଷର ମହିଳା ରାସ୍ତା ବ୍ୟବସାୟୀ। ତାଙ୍କର 2 ମାସ ଧରି କାଶ ସହିତ କଫ ହେଉଛି, ସନ୍ଧ୍ୟାରେ ସାମାନ୍ୟ ଜ୍ୱର ହେଉଛି ଏବଂ ଓଜନ ବହୁତ କମିଯାଇଛି। ସେ ରାତିରେ ଝାଳ ବୋହୁଥିବା କଥା କହୁଛନ୍ତି। ପୂର୍ବ ମେଡିକାଲ୍ ଇତିହାସ କିଛି ନାହିଁ, କିନ୍ତୁ ଗତ ଏକ ବର୍ଷ ଧରି ତାଙ୍କୁ ବାରମ୍ବାର ଶୋଷ ଲାଗୁଛି ଏବଂ ପରିସ୍ରା ଅଧିକ ହେଉଛି। ପରୀକ୍ଷା ସମୟରେ ସେ ପତଳା ଅଛନ୍ତି, BMI 16.5 kg/m2। ଛାତି ପରୀକ୍ଷା କଲେ ବାମ ପାର୍ଶ୍ୱରେ ସୂକ୍ଷ୍ମ ଶବ୍ଦ ଶୁଣାଯାଉଛି। ରକ୍ତ ଶର୍କରା random 210 mg/dL। କଫ ପରୀକ୍ଷା ପାଇଁ ପଠାଯାଇଛି। HbA1c ପରୀକ୍ଷା ପାଇଁ ନିର୍ଦ୍ଦେଶ ଦିଆଯାଇଛି।
